title: NCP-GENL · Monitoring & Reliability
description: Latency SLOs, drift detection, quality regression, logging, cost, reliability patterns (exam domain, 7%).

cards.front: Which two latency metrics are standard for measuring streaming LLM responses?
cards.back: Time-to-First-Token (TTFT), dominated by the prefill phase, and Inter-Token-Latency (ITL), dominated by decode. TTFT controls perceived responsiveness; ITL controls steady-state speed once tokens start flowing. Track p50/p95/p99 of each, segmented by prompt length and concurrency, and set SLOs against them — they have different optimization targets.
cards.quizAnswer: Time-to-First-Token (prefill-dominated) and Inter-Token-Latency (decode-dominated); track p50/p95/p99 by prompt length and concurrency.
distractors: Total throughput and disk I/O, since end-to-end tokens per second and storage bandwidth together bound response speed.
TTL and DNS-resolution time, which together determine how quickly a streaming connection reaches the inference server.
Time-to-First-Token and total request duration, since the gap between them captures everything about decode speed.
p50 latency only, measured end-to-end, because tail percentiles are too noisy to set meaningful streaming SLOs.
Queue-wait time and GPU temperature, since rising temperature is the leading indicator of degraded streaming latency.
Prefill FLOPs and decode FLOPs, which directly determine the two phases' latencies independent of the hardware.

cards.front: Which signals detect LLM quality regression in production without ground-truth labels?
cards.back: User-satisfaction proxies (thumbs, edit distance to user revisions, retry rate, abandonment), prediction-distribution drift, refusal rate, and judge-LLM scores on sampled traffic. Labels arrive late or never, so these leading indicators catch regressions early. Pair them with a held-out canonical eval suite that runs on every release.
cards.quizAnswer: Satisfaction proxies (thumbs, edit distance, retry, abandonment), output drift, refusal rate, and judge-LLM scores on sampled traffic.
distractors: GPU temperature trends only, since thermal throttling is the earliest sign that answer quality has started to degrade.
Disk free space on the inference nodes, because a full disk silently truncates responses and lowers their quality.
The cluster's hostname and node labels, which reveal whether requests landed on an older, lower-quality model replica.
Satisfaction proxies and refusal rate, but never judge-LLM scores, since an automated judge cannot run without labels.
Raw request volume per hour, since a sudden traffic drop is the strongest indicator that users find answers worse.
Average response length over time, since regressions always manifest as the model producing noticeably shorter answers.

cards.front: What is the recommended approach to detect data drift in an LLM application's input distribution?
cards.back: Compare token-distribution and length statistics, embedding-cluster shifts, topic distribution, and language ID over rolling windows against a baseline, using distributional distances like KS test or PSI. Drift in user prompts is normal but matters: log embeddings/feature stats, compute the distances, alert on threshold breaches, and trigger investigation or retraining.
cards.quizAnswer: Compare token/length stats, embedding shifts, topic, and language over rolling windows to a baseline via KS test or PSI.
distractors: Ignore input drift entirely, since modern LLMs generalize across distributions and only output drift affects quality.
Restart the model nightly regardless of any measurements, since a fresh process resets any accumulated input skew.
Wait for user complaints before investigating, then compare the recent inputs against the original training distribution.
Compare embedding shifts over rolling windows, but trigger retraining automatically on every shift without any threshold.
Track only the average prompt length, since length is the single feature that fully characterizes input distribution drift.
Compare the model's output distribution against its inputs, since input drift is always mirrored by a shift in outputs.

cards.front: Which logging policy best supports incident debugging while respecting privacy?
cards.back: Log full prompts and responses with PII redaction, plus model/version IDs, retrieved chunk IDs, latency, and hashed user/session IDs; retain per policy and access via least privilege. The goal is enough metadata to reproduce an incident, redacted enough to comply. Retention and access controls (SOC 2 / GDPR) are non-negotiable in regulated industries.
cards.quizAnswer: Log PII-redacted prompts/responses with model version, chunk IDs, latency, and hashed user IDs; retain per policy, least-privilege access.
distractors: Log nothing about request contents, since any stored prompt or response is a privacy liability that prevents compliance.
Log full prompts and responses in clear text indefinitely, since complete unredacted history is essential for debugging.
Log only random samples of requests with no metadata, so storage stays small and no single user can be re-identified.
Log PII-redacted prompts but discard all model and version IDs, since those reveal proprietary deployment details.
Log everything in clear text but encrypt the disk, since at-rest encryption satisfies GDPR without needing redaction.
Log only the latency and status code per request, omitting contents entirely, since reproductions can be reconstructed from timing.

cards.front: Which metric is most appropriate for tracking the cost efficiency of an LLM service?
cards.back: Cost per 1k tokens (input and output, separately) and cost per resolved task, broken down by model, request class, and user segment. Token-level pricing is the right unit, and per-task ROI captures whether the service actually solves user problems. Cost dashboards by model and route reveal where cascading, caching, distillation, or routing changes pay off.
cards.quizAnswer: Cost per 1k tokens (input/output separately) and cost per resolved task, broken down by model, request class, and segment.
distractors: CPU temperature of the inference nodes, since thermal headroom determines how many requests a server can process per dollar.
The rendered HTML page size of the chat UI, since smaller payloads reduce the bandwidth cost of serving each response.
The number of GitHub stars on the serving framework, since more popular runtimes are better optimized and cheaper to run.
Cost per request regardless of length, since flat per-call pricing is simpler than tracking input and output tokens separately.
Total monthly GPU spend alone, since a single aggregate figure is sufficient to compare the efficiency of different models.
Cost per 1k tokens combined into one input-plus-output figure, since separating the two adds complexity without insight.

cards.front: What do Triton's queue-duration and compute-infer-duration metrics each tell you, and how are they used together?
cards.back: nv_inference_queue_duration_us is time a request spends waiting in the scheduling queue; nv_inference_compute_infer_duration_us is the actual model compute time. A high queue-to-compute ratio means requests are backing up faster than the GPU can serve them — the signal to scale out, raise instance count, or enable in-flight batching. Low queue with high compute means the model itself is the bottleneck.
cards.quizAnswer: Queue duration is scheduling wait; compute-infer duration is model compute. A high ratio means back-pressure — scale out or batch.
distractors: Queue duration is the model compute time and compute-infer duration is the scheduling wait; a high ratio means the GPU is idle.
Both measure end-to-end latency from different ports, so subtracting them gives the network round-trip time per request.
Queue duration counts failed requests and compute-infer counts successful ones; their ratio is the service's error rate.
Queue duration is the KV-cache eviction time and compute-infer is the prefill time; together they decompose TTFT.
Queue duration is wait time and compute-infer is compute, but a high ratio means the model is too large and must be quantized.
Both measure the same compute phase at p50 and p99; comparing them reveals the tail latency of the model's forward pass.

cards.front: How do readiness and liveness probes differ for a Triton deployment on Kubernetes?
cards.back: The readiness probe (/v2/health/ready) gates traffic — Kubernetes only routes requests to a pod once it reports ready, e.g., after the model has loaded. The liveness probe (/v2/health/live) detects a hung process and restarts the pod when it stops responding. Readiness controls whether to send traffic; liveness controls whether to restart.
cards.quizAnswer: Readiness (/v2/health/ready) gates traffic until the model loads; liveness (/v2/health/live) restarts a hung pod.
distractors: Readiness restarts a hung pod once the model stops responding, while liveness gates traffic until the model finishes loading.
Both probes gate traffic; readiness checks the HTTP port and liveness checks the gRPC port, so both must pass to serve.
Readiness measures GPU memory headroom and liveness measures GPU temperature, throttling traffic when either is exceeded.
Readiness gates traffic until the model loads, while liveness loads the next model version for zero-downtime rollouts.
Liveness gates traffic and readiness restarts the pod, which is why a failing readiness probe triggers an immediate reboot.
Both probes restart the pod on failure; they differ only in how frequently Kubernetes polls each health endpoint.

cards.front: What does graceful degradation provide as a reliability pattern for LLM serving?
cards.back: When the primary model is overloaded or failing, the system falls back to a cheaper smaller model, a cached response, or a simpler template instead of erroring out — preserving availability at reduced quality. It is paired with circuit breakers (stop sending to a failing backend and retry with backoff) so transient failures don't cascade into a full outage.
cards.quizAnswer: Under overload or failure, fall back to a smaller model, cached response, or template — preserving availability at reduced quality.
distractors: Under overload, queue all requests indefinitely until the primary model frees up, so no request is ever served degraded output.
Under failure, immediately return an error to the client, since serving a lower-quality answer is worse than no answer.
Under overload, upgrade requests to a larger model with more capacity, trading higher cost for guaranteed availability.
Under failure, fall back to a smaller model, but only for requests that have already exceeded their latency SLA budget.
Under overload, shed load by dropping a random fraction of requests, so the remaining ones always get full-quality answers.
Under failure, replay the failed requests against every backend simultaneously and return whichever responds first.

cards.front: What does the Triton Model Analyzer automate?
cards.back: It sweeps serving configurations — batch size, model instance count, and concurrency — and benchmarks them to recommend the setup that best meets your throughput and latency targets. Instead of hand-tuning config.pbtxt by trial and error, you let the analyzer search the space and surface the Pareto-optimal configurations for your SLA.
cards.quizAnswer: It sweeps batch size, instance count, and concurrency, benchmarking each to recommend configs that meet throughput/latency targets.
distractors: It analyzes the model's weights to recommend the optimal quantization precision for the target GPU's Tensor Cores.
It traces production traffic and automatically rewrites the model ensemble to remove redundant preprocessing stages.
It sweeps learning rate and batch size during fine-tuning to recommend the configuration with the lowest validation loss.
It monitors the queue-to-compute ratio at runtime and dynamically adjusts the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler thresholds.
It sweeps batch size and instance count, but optimizes only for maximum throughput, ignoring any latency constraints.
It profiles individual GPU kernels and recommends which layers to fuse to reduce the model's memory bandwidth usage.
